content.text: Refactoring agentic AI integration code requires discipline that goes beyond ordinary software refactoring. In conventional code, a refactoring that preserves unit-test outcomes is considered safe. In agentic AI integration code, a refactoring can change the exact string passed to a language model—changing how context is assembled, how tool instructions are formatted, or which prompt version is selected—without changing any observable test behavior, yet producing measurably different model output in production. This makes behavioral preservation, verified through evaluation suites, the primary safety gate for refactoring AI integration code.

Refactoring discipline for agentic AI integration code is a structured approach to code improvement that adds AI-specific safety constraints to the standard refactoring cycle. The core constraint is the behavioral preservation invariant: no refactoring may change the string that is ultimately passed to the language model without explicit acknowledgment and evaluation validation. A code refactoring that happens to change the prompt—even superficially (whitespace, variable interpolation order, f-string concatenation reordering)—is actually a combined code + prompt change and must be treated as such.

The practice mandates three things beyond standard refactoring: running the evaluation suite before the refactoring to record a behavioral baseline, running it after to verify the baseline is unchanged, and rejecting the refactoring if any evaluation metric shifts by more than a threshold (typically 1-2%). These constraints ensure that code improvements do not silently degrade model behavior.

LLMs are exquisitely sensitive to prompt text. Adding a trailing space to a system message, reordering sentences in a tool-calling instruction, or changing how few-shot examples are concatenated can all produce measurably different output distributions. Because these effects are probabilistic rather than deterministic, standard unit tests—which compare exact return values—cannot detect them. A unit test that mocks the LLM response will pass regardless of how the prompt string changed.

This creates a unique risk in agentic AI refactoring: a developer extracts a prompt-building helper function, reorders the message assembly, and commits a clean refactoring PR. The unit tests pass. CI is green. The change ships. Two days later, the evaluation dashboard shows a 4% drop in tool-calling accuracy because the reordered context shifted the model's attention in a way that degraded instruction following. Without eval-guarded refactoring, this connection is invisible.
Behavioral safety: eval-guarded refactoring guarantees that code improvements do not silently degrade model behavior.
Confidence in refactoring: developers can modernise AI integration code without fear of undetected behavioral regressions.
Separation of concerns: the discipline forces clean separation between prompt content (not to be touched in a refactor) and code structure (safe to improve).
Cleaner architecture: the discipline's requirement to extract LLM clients and prompt registries naturally produces more testable, maintainable code.
Audit trail: eval-guarded refactors produce a before/after evaluation record that is retained alongside the commit.
Prevents scope creep: the 'no prompt changes during refactor' rule prevents refactoring PRs from inadvertently containing prompt improvements.
Evaluation cost: running the full evaluation suite before and after a refactoring doubles the evaluation cost for that PR.
Slower refactoring cycle: the mandatory evaluation round-trips add time to the refactoring process.
Evaluation flakiness: if the evaluation suite has non-deterministic results (which LLM-as-judge evals often do), borderline score deltas are hard to interpret.
Scope discipline required: developers must resist the temptation to 'improve the prompt a little' while they have the code open for refactoring.
Incomplete evaluation coverage: if the evaluation suite does not cover all dimensions of model behavior, a behavioral regression in an uncovered dimension will not be caught.
Eval-guarded refactoring follows a five-step protocol:
Run the evaluation suite against the current main branch and record the baseline scores in a JSON artifact.
Apply the code refactoring, touching only code structure—not prompt strings, not evaluation logic, not context assembly order.
Run the evaluation suite again against the refactored code.
Compare the two evaluation artifacts: if any metric has shifted by more than the tolerance threshold (1% for accuracy metrics, 5ms for latency), investigate the delta before proceeding.
Merge the refactoring only when the delta is within tolerance. Attach the baseline and post-refactor evaluation artifacts to the PR description.
The 'no prompt changes during refactor' rule is enforced by the commit convention: refactoring commits use the
type, which triggers a CI check that diffs the prompts/ directory and fails if any prompt file was modified in the commit. This prevents accidental prompt changes from hiding inside refactoring PRs.

attrs.alt: Before/after diagram of refactoring agentic AI integration code, showing coupling removal and eval-guarded flow

content.text: The refactoring target in agentic AI code is the decoupling of three concerns that are typically entangled: the LLM client (which should be an injected interface), the prompt content (which should live in a registry, not in code), and the orchestration logic (which should coordinate without embedding prompt text). Achieving this separation requires multiple incremental refactoring steps, each guarded by an evaluation run.
Before any significant refactoring of code that assembles prompts, builds context windows, or calls the LLM API.
When extracting LLM client instantiation from business logic classes.
When moving hardcoded prompts from Python f-strings to a versioned prompt registry.
When modernising tool-calling code to a new API version (e.g., from legacy to new tool schema format).
When decomposing a monolithic agent class into smaller, composable components.

# scripts/eval-baseline.py
# Run before and after a refactoring to compare behavioral baselines
import json
import time
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

import anthropic

EVAL_CASES = [
    {"input": "What is the capital of France?", "expected": "Paris"},
    {"input": "Summarise the attached document in 3 bullets.", "expected_type": "bullet_list"},
    # ... load full suite from eval/ directory
]

def run_eval_suite(prompt_version: str, model: str = "claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219") -> dict[str, Any]:
    """Run the evaluation suite and return aggregated scores."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    results = []
    for case in EVAL_CASES:
        start = time.perf_counter()
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=f"prompt_version={prompt_version}",  # injected for test
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
        )
        latency_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        output = resp.content[0].text
        # Simple correctness check (real eval uses LLM-as-judge or exact match)
        correct = case.get("expected", "") in output if "expected" in case else True
        results.append({"correct": correct, "latency_ms": latency_ms})
    
    accuracy = sum(r["correct"] for r in results) / len(results)
    p99_latency = sorted(r["latency_ms"] for r in results)[int(len(results) * 0.99)]
    return {"accuracy": accuracy, "p99_latency_ms": p99_latency, "n": len(results)}

def compare_baselines(before: dict, after: dict, tolerance: float = 0.01) -> bool:
    """Return True if the refactoring is safe (no behavioral regression)."""
    delta = after["accuracy"] - before["accuracy"]
    print(f"Accuracy: {before['accuracy']:.3f} → {after['accuracy']:.3f} (Δ={delta:+.3f})")
    if abs(delta) > tolerance:
        print(f"WARNING: accuracy delta {delta:.3f} exceeds tolerance {tolerance}")
        return False
    print("Baseline comparison: PASS")
    return True

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    phase = sys.argv[1]  # 'before' or 'after'
    prompt_version = sys.argv[2]
    scores = run_eval_suite(prompt_version)
    Path(f"eval-baseline-{phase}.json").write_text(json.dumps(scores, indent=2))
    print(f"Saved {phase} baseline: {scores}")

# Refactoring workflow script
#!/bin/bash
set -e

echo "=== Step 1: Record pre-refactor baseline ==="
python3 scripts/eval-baseline.py before "prompts/current-version"

echo "=== Step 2: Apply refactoring ==="
# (Developer applies code changes here)
# Rule: no changes to files in prompts/ directory
echo "Checking prompts/ directory is unchanged..."
git diff --name-only HEAD -- prompts/ | grep -q . && {
  echo "ERROR: Refactoring PR must not modify prompt files"
  exit 1
}

echo "=== Step 3: Record post-refactor baseline ==="
python3 scripts/eval-baseline.py after "prompts/current-version"

echo "=== Step 4: Compare baselines ==="
python3 -c "
import json
before = json.load(open('eval-baseline-before.json'))
after = json.load(open('eval-baseline-after.json'))
delta = after['accuracy'] - before['accuracy']
print(f'Accuracy delta: {delta:+.4f}')
if abs(delta) > 0.01:
    print('FAIL: behavioral regression detected')
    exit(1)
print('PASS: no behavioral regression')
"
echo "Refactoring is safe to merge.
Three common refactoring patterns in agentic AI code and their eval-guarded application:
replace 'anthropic.Anthropic()' direct instantiation with an injected 'LLMClient' protocol, enabling mock injection in tests. This changes zero prompt bytes and should produce a zero eval delta.
extract f-string prompt text into a versioned YAML file and replace the f-string with a registry lookup. This must preserve the exact prompt string byte-for-byte; eval validates it.

split a 500-line AgentPipeline class into Planner, Executor, and Responder. The refactoring preserves the exact message sequence passed to the model; eval validates that decomposition did not change ordering.

A search AI company had a single 800-line Python file containing both their RAG pipeline logic and the prompt templates hardcoded as multi-line f-strings. A senior engineer proposed a refactoring to separate concerns: extract prompts to YAML, move the LLM client to a dependency-injected abstraction, and split the pipeline into composable stages.
The refactoring was done in three PRs, each with a full eval run before and after:
PR 1: Extract LLM client to an injected interface (eval delta: 0%).
PR 2: Move prompts from f-strings to YAML registry (eval delta: 0.3% — investigated and found a trailing newline difference in one prompt; fixed and re-ran to 0%).
PR 3: Decompose pipeline into composable stages (eval delta: 0%).
The trailing newline discovery in PR 2 was exactly the kind of subtle prompt change that would have shipped undetected without eval-guarded refactoring.
Changing prompt text during a refactoring PR:
a refactoring is a structural improvement; prompt improvements are a separate concern. Mix them and you cannot attribute an eval delta to its cause.
unit tests with mocked LLM responses cannot detect prompt string changes. Always run the real evaluation suite against the real model.
Using a tolerance that is too wide:
a 5% tolerance for an accuracy metric would allow a significant behavioral regression to pass as an acceptable refactoring. Use 1% for primary metrics.
Refactoring and adding features in the same PR:
a combined refactor-and-feature PR cannot be safely eval-guarded because any eval delta could come from either the refactoring or the feature.
Adopt the 'single concern per PR' rule: refactorings and behavior changes never share a PR.
Store the pre- and post-refactor evaluation artifacts in the PR description or as CI job attachments so reviewers can see the behavioral evidence.
Use a CI step that fails if any file in prompts/ is modified in a commit typed as 'refactor'.
Keep the evaluation suite deterministic where possible (exact-match evals, or LLM-as-judge with temperature=0) to make baseline comparisons reliable.
Perform refactoring in small, incremental steps; large refactorings are harder to eval-guard because the source of any delta is ambiguous.
Document the eval baseline protocol in CONTRIBUTING.md so all contributors know to run it before submitting a refactoring PR.

Running the full evaluation suite twice per refactoring PR is the main performance cost. For large evaluation suites (500+ cases), this can take 30-60 minutes and incur significant API costs. Mitigate with a stratified sample: run a 50-case 'quick eval' as a fast gate, and run the full suite only if the quick eval passes. The quick eval should cover all key behavioral dimensions in proportion to their importance.
Lint for prompt changes in refactor commits
Detect prompt file changes in refactor PR
Eval-guarded refactoring improves security by preventing accidental removal of safety guardrails embedded in prompt text during code cleanup. The requirement that no prompt file change during a
-typed commit creates an automatic audit trail: any prompt change is always a deliberate, typed commit that triggers the appropriate review gates.

Reliability is improved because developers can confidently refactor AI integration code knowing that behavioral regressions will be caught before merge. This removes the fear that slows down code improvement in AI systems, enabling teams to maintain architectural cleanliness as the codebase grows.
AI integration code refactoring must be guarded by evaluation suite runs before and after, not just unit tests.
The behavioral preservation invariant: no refactoring may change the string ultimately passed to the language model.
Enforce 'no prompt changes in refactor commits' via a CI check that diffs the prompts/ directory.
Run the evaluation suite as a before/after comparison and fail the merge if any metric shifts more than 1%.
Separate refactoring PRs from feature or prompt change PRs so eval deltas are attributable to a single cause.
Small, incremental refactorings are safer and easier to eval-guard than large, sweeping restructuring changes.
